library_metadata.name: Suryadip Ghoshal — Prompt Template Library v1.0

templates.name: Structured Document Forge
templates.custom_role: Senior consultant at a tier-1 AI advisory firm who writes with the precision of an in-house lawyer and the persuasion of a McKinsey engagement manager
templates.context:
I am {{SURYA_ROLE}} at {{COMPANY}} ({{COMPANY_CONTEXT}}).
The document I need is for {{AUDIENCE}} — their sophistication level is {{AUDIENCE_LEVEL}}.
Source materials I am providing: {{SOURCE_MATERIALS}}.
This document will be used for {{PURPOSE}}: {{PURPOSE_DETAIL}}.
Source of truth: {{SOURCE_DOCUMENT}} — where my documents conflict with your training data, defer to my documents.
templates.task:
Create a professional {{DOCUMENT_TYPE}} covering the following sections:
{{SECTION_LIST}}

The reader's primary question is: {{READER_QUESTION}}.
The document should answer it by page/section {{TARGET_SECTION}}.

Output format: {{FORMAT}} (Word .docx / PPTX / React HTML / PDF).
File save location: {{OUTPUT_PATH}}.
Structure conventions: {{STRUCTURE_CONVENTIONS}}.
Tone: {{TONE}} — {{TONE_DETAIL}}.
templates.constraints:
- Deliverable must be a downloadable file, not inline text.
- Sections must be clearly labeled with headers.
- No filler content; every paragraph must carry information.
- Tables preferred over bullet lists for comparative information.
- If pricing or client names are included, flag them for editorial review.
- Word count target: {{WORD_COUNT}} or {{SLIDE_COUNT}} slides.
templates.audience: {{AUDIENCE}} — e.g., CAMS CEO, BharatPe audit team, UCO Bank tender committee. Sophistication: {{SOPHISTICATION_LEVEL}}. They are skeptical of: {{SKEPTICISM_POINTS}}.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Create a Word document for the UCO Bank RFP covering: Executive Summary, Technical Approach, ECLLens Platform Overview, Team Composition, and a Gantt-style timeline. Primary reader: QCBS evaluation committee. They care about methodology rigor, not marketing.'

Example 2: 'Build a 6-section service offering doc for Third Party Model Validation to be posted on think360.ai — no client names, professional tone, ₹2L/model pricing. Reader: BFSI CTO browsing the website for 90 seconds.'
templates.optional:
After producing the document, flag:
1. Any claims that require client-specific evidence before publishing
2. Any pricing or competitive statements that need legal review
3. Editorial decisions you made that I should be aware of
4. Sentences that a skeptical buyer would challenge
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- All sections present and non-empty
- Output format matches specification (file, not inline)
- Zero placeholder text remaining
- Reader's primary question answered within the target section
- No unsupported claims without evidence flags
trigger_phrases: I need a service offering doc that...
Turn these notes into a professional...
templates.success_criteria: Downloadable file, correct section count, no placeholder text, flags editorial decisions, answers the reader's question by the target section
common_follow_ups: Change the tone in Section 3 — it reads too salesy
Add a pricing table
Can we do this in HTML instead of Word?
anti_patterns: Not specifying output format upfront (leads to inline text instead of file)
Omitting audience — changes tone dramatically
Providing source material in a single wall of text with no structure
Not designating source of truth — Claude mixes training data with your docs

templates.name: Strategic Situation Brief
templates.custom_role: Senior M&A advisor and negotiation strategist with deep India corporate law and accounting familiarity. Thinks in leverage maps and scenario trees, not platitudes.
templates.context:
The situation: {{SITUATION_SUMMARY}}.
Key parties: {{PARTY_MAP}} — their motivations are {{MOTIVATION_ANALYSIS}}.
Contractual/legal anchors: {{CONTRACT_REFS}}.
My position: {{MY_POSITION}}.
Their likely position: {{THEIR_POSITION}}.
Time pressure: {{TIMELINE}}.
Stakes: {{STAKES}}.
Source of truth: {{CONTRACT_NAME}} — where the contract text conflicts with your legal training, defer to the contract.
templates.task:
Produce a strategic brief covering:
1. Situation map — who holds what leverage and why
2. Scenario playbook — {{N}} scenarios ranked by probability
3. My optimal opening position and fallback positions
4. Red lines I must not cross
5. Key arguments and counter-arguments
6. The strongest counter-argument AGAINST my position — and how to neutralize it
7. Recommended communication approach for {{CHANNEL}}

Format as: {{FORMAT}} — tables preferred for scenario matrix.
templates.constraints:
- Be direct. Do not hedge every statement.
- Flag when my position has legal vs. factual vs. negotiation support.
- Distinguish what I know from what I'm inferring.
- Do not recommend disclosing information I haven't said I'm willing to share.
- If you're uncertain about a legal interpretation, say so — do not paper over gaps.
templates.audience: Myself — strategic prep document, not for sharing. May brief co-founder Amit Das.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'CAMS is trying to reduce the balance tranche payout from ₹100 Cr to something lower. The SHA has an unconditional floor at Clause 2.3. Their CFO is rational and number-driven. Build a negotiation matrix and scenario playbook for the meeting on March 20th.'

Example 2: 'BharatPe wants to renegotiate the CAC amortization timeline. They'll cite merchant churn data. Our contract says X. Build the counter-strategy.'
templates.optional:
After the brief, separately flag:
- Any accounting standards (Ind AS) they might cite to undermine my position
- Any language in the documents I provided that cuts against me
- A one-paragraph 'if I could only say one thing in this meeting' summary
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Scenario matrix with probability estimates present
- Leverage map identifies information asymmetries
- At least one counter-argument against my own position included
- Specific clause citations (not generic legal principles)
- Communication templates are ready to send, not abstract advice
trigger_phrases: I have a critical meeting with...
Help me prepare for a negotiation around...
Build a strategy for the upcoming discussion with...
Given the SHA/SPA/contract, what's my position?
templates.success_criteria: Scenario matrix with probabilities, leverage map, specific opening and fallback positions, adversarial argument addressed, communication templates
common_follow_ups: What if they come in with impairment-based accounting arguments?
Draft the WhatsApp message to the CFO
What's our strongest counter if they claim the floor is conditional?
anti_patterns: Not providing the actual contract text or clause references
Asking for strategy without sharing counterparty motivation
Wanting diplomatic framing — this pattern works best with direct, frank analysis
Missing source of truth designation — leads to confident statements on points where the contract should take precedence

templates.name: Role-Calibrated Deep Research
templates.custom_role: {{EXPERT_ROLE}} — e.g., Senior Macroeconomic Research Director, Fundamental Equity Analyst (Value Investing), VC analyst, Industry analyst. Writes institutional-grade research, not blog posts.
templates.context:
Research scope: {{TOPIC}}.
Why I need this: {{PURPOSE}} — e.g., investment thesis, BD preparation, thought leadership, board briefing.
Level of rigor required: {{RIGOR_LEVEL}} — institutional / practitioner / exploratory.
Data sources to prioritize: {{SOURCE_PREFERENCES}} — e.g., CMIE, RBI, SEBI, company filings, Bloomberg.
Time horizon: {{TIME_SCOPE}}.
Initial thesis or hypothesis (if any): {{INITIAL_VIEW}}.
templates.task:
Produce a comprehensive research report on {{TOPIC}} covering:
{{COVERAGE_AREAS}}

For each major claim, provide:
- The data or evidence
- Confidence level (high / medium / low)
- Whether this is structural or cyclical

If this is an investment analysis, include:
- Business model & moat assessment
- Unit economics / financial analysis
- Competitive landscape
- Valuation drivers (DCF framework + multiples if applicable)
- Risk factors (regulatory, competitive, macro)

Conclude with: {{CONCLUSION_TYPE}} — e.g., investment recommendation, hypothesis verdict, strategic implication.

Minimum depth: {{WORD_COUNT}} words.
templates.constraints:
- Use institutional data sources: {{SOURCE_LIST}}.
- Distinguish structural from cyclical changes.
- No motivational framing. Be a truth-teller.
- If data is unavailable, say so rather than approximate.
- Board/investment committee tone — insight-heavy, not descriptive.
- Include 'so what' after every major finding.
- Do NOT output final financial advice; provide the framework for decisions.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Act as a Senior Macroeconomic Research Director. Research India's K-shaped economy across five hypotheses: white-collar recession, negative real wages, K-shaped consumption, household debt stress, manufacturing cost disadvantages. Minimum 10,000 words. Use CMIE, RBI, SEBI data.'

Example 2: 'Research GV (Google Ventures). Cover: investment philosophy, portfolio, team, differentiators. I'm evaluating them as a potential investor in Think360.'

Example 3: 'Act as a Fundamental Equity Analyst. Build an investment framework for {{COMPANY}} using Porter's 5 Forces for moat assessment and DCF principles for valuation drivers.'
templates.optional:
At the end, add:
1. 'Strategic Relevance for Think360.ai / Surya' — what specifically from this research is actionable for my context
2. Sensitivity scenarios — what changes if key assumptions break
3. One-page executive summary suitable for board distribution
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Every major claim has evidence and confidence level
- Structural vs. cyclical distinction applied throughout
- 'So what' present after major findings
- Minimum depth threshold met
- Sources cited (not just training data assertions)
- Strategic relevance section connects to my context
trigger_phrases: Act as a [expert role] and research...
Give me a comprehensive analysis of...
What is the fundamental thesis for...
templates.success_criteria: Evidence-backed claims with confidence levels, structural/cyclical distinction, strategic implication section, minimum depth met, decision-ready output
common_follow_ups: Now turn this into a BCG-style deck
Convert to a white paper for public distribution
What's the investment implication of hypothesis 3?
Compare this specific metric against [Competitor]
anti_patterns: Not specifying rigor level — leads to surface-level outputs
Omitting 'truth-teller' instruction — produces hedged, diplomatic analysis
No source preferences — Claude defaults to general internet instead of institutional data
Asking for a final answer on complex valuation without forcing show-your-work

templates.name: PRD / Offering Document Builder
templates.custom_role: Senior Staff Product Manager specializing in AI-native Enterprise SaaS, with deep domain expertise in {{DOMAIN}}. Writes for both technical and business audiences.
templates.context:
Product: {{PRODUCT_NAME}} (e.g., InquiroAI, KYC Co-Pilot, ECLLens, Algo360).
Company: Think360.ai — {{COMPANY_CONTEXT}}.
Problem being solved: {{PROBLEM_STATEMENT}}.
Target buyer: {{BUYER_PERSONA}} — their sophistication is {{SOPHISTICATION}}.
What they're skeptical of: {{BUYER_SKEPTICISM}}.
Competitive context: {{COMPETITIVE_CONTEXT}}.
Existing artifacts I'm providing: {{SOURCE_ARTIFACTS}}.
Document subtype: {{SUBTYPE}} — PRD / offering doc / pitch doc / feature spec / technical proposal / RFP response.
templates.task:
Build a complete {{DOCUMENT_TYPE}} covering:
{{SECTION_LIST}}

Format: {{FORMAT}} — Markdown / Word .docx / HTML.
Length: {{LENGTH}}.

If PRD: include User Journeys, Core Epics & Stories, Data & ML Requirements, Non-Functional Requirements.
If Offering Doc: include Value Proposition, Methodology, Platform Overview, Pricing, Differentiators.
If RFP Response: include Self-Assessment Score, Team Composition, Timeline.

The reader's primary question is: {{READER_QUESTION}}.
templates.constraints:
- Named products (Algo360, ECLLens, KwikID) must be evidence, not advertising.
- Avoid generic benefit statements — anchor every claim to a mechanism or outcome.
- If pricing is included, note it requires review before publishing.
- User stories in 'As a [persona], I want [capability] so that [outcome]' format.
- Technical sections must be specific enough for engineering estimation.
- Include a 'Technical Governance' checklist for enterprise compliance.
- No filler content — every section must carry information.
templates.audience: {{AUDIENCE}} — e.g., BFSI CTO, RFP committee, Think360 product team, website visitor. Primary reader: {{PRIMARY_READER_ROLE}}, sophistication: {{LEVEL}}, skeptical of: {{SKEPTICISM}}.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Build a full technical proposal for the UCO Bank ECL RFP. Include: methodology, ECLLens platform overview, team composition, self-assessment score against QCBS matrix. Primary reader: QCBS evaluation committee.'

Example 2: 'Create a service offering doc for Third Party Model Validation, suitable for the think360.ai website. 13 sections. Word format. Reader: BFSI CTO browsing for 90 seconds.'

Example 3: 'Create a detailed PRD for InquiroAI Layer 5 (Obsidian Knowledge Graph). Include user journeys, epics, technical requirements, and data schema. Reader: Engineering lead who needs to estimate sprint effort.'
templates.optional:
After the main document, add a meta-note:
1. Content gaps that need real data before going live
2. Claims a skeptical buyer would challenge
3. What to cut if the reader only has 5 minutes
4. Monetization or ROI drivers for the enterprise buyer
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Complete section coverage — no placeholder sections
- Buyer-specific language (not generic)
- Mechanism-anchored claims (not benefit statements)
- Engineering-estimable technical sections
- Reader's primary question answered
- Can be handed to engineering or client with zero structural editing
trigger_phrases: I need a feature spec for...
templates.success_criteria: Immediately usable by the target reader without clarification, no placeholder sections, buyer-specific language, mechanism-anchored claims
common_follow_ups: Make Section 4 more technical for a CTO audience
Add a pricing table with tiered options
Now convert this to a slide deck
Break down Epic 2 into JIRA-ready stories
anti_patterns: No audience specification — product docs for BFSI CTOs vs. website visitors are very different
Providing too little context on the problem being solved
Not specifying what artifacts you already have (leads to Claude inventing sections you already wrote)
Writing generic marketing-speak instead of technical requirements

templates.name: AI Systems & Agent Architecture

variants.description: Full system design for multi-agent or AI-native applications
variants.custom_role: Principal AI Systems Architect who designs production-grade agentic systems — thinks in failure modes, not happy paths
variants.context:
System: {{SYSTEM_NAME}} (e.g., InquiroAI, TrustMesh, rAIdar, OrgOS).
Use case: {{USE_CASE}}.
Data sources: {{DATA_SOURCES}}.
Constraints: {{CONSTRAINTS}} (latency, cost, compliance, infra).
Infra environment: {{INFRA}} (cloud provider, existing stack).
Compliance standards: {{COMPLIANCE}} (SOC2, DPDPA, RBI guidelines).
variants.task:
Design the full system architecture covering:
1. System context diagram (textual or Mermaid.js)
2. Core components & services — what each does, why it exists
3. Agent roles and responsibilities (if multi-agent)
4. Inter-agent communication protocol
5. Data flow — from ingestion to output
6. Model selection rationale — which model for which task and why
7. Failure modes and fallback chains
8. Security & compliance governance layer
9. MVP vs. scale architecture (what to build first, what to defer)
10. Cost model — estimated per-request and monthly costs
variants.constraints:
- Practical and buildable — not a whiteboard fantasy.
- Avoid buzzwords without specifics.
- Include trade-offs for every major decision.
- Address how the AI/agentic layer interacts with deterministic data layer without hallucination.
- Multi-tenancy controls if applicable.
- Distinguish between orchestration logic and business logic.
variants.examples:
Example 1: 'Design the architecture for InquiroAI: React + Express + PostgreSQL frontend/backend, OpenRouter as the sole AI gateway, 6 layers (project entity, prompt intelligence, context compression, Notion memory, Obsidian knowledge graph). Show how layers compose.'

Example 2: 'Design TrustMesh as an Agent Identity Protocol. Components: identity issuance, attestation verification, trust scoring, audit trail. Must handle 10K verifications/sec at sub-200ms latency.'

Example 3: 'Design rAIdar ad compliance surveillance. Input: 500K+ ad creatives/day across media. Output: violation flags with regulation citations. Must handle multimodal (image+text+video).'

variants.description: Craft robust, instruction-following system prompts for AI agents
variants.custom_role: Lead Prompt Engineer and Agentic AI Architect who designs prompts as contracts, not suggestions
variants.context:
Agent: {{AGENT_NAME}} within {{SYSTEM_NAME}}.
Core function: {{CORE_FUNCTION}} (e.g., KYC document parsing, medical conference synthesis, regulatory gap detection).
Input data: {{INPUT_DATA}}.
Expected output: {{EXPECTED_OUTPUT}}.
Downstream consumers: {{CONSUMERS}} (other agents, APIs, human reviewers).
variants.task:
Draft a production-grade system prompt for this agent including:
1. Role Definition — who this agent is and isn't
2. Context — what it knows about the system
3. Step-by-step task execution protocol
4. Strict constraints and guardrails (what it MUST NOT do)
5. Output format specification (JSON schema, markdown structure, etc.)
6. Error handling — what to do when input is malformed or ambiguous
7. Tone and communication style
8. Chain-of-thought directives where needed

Output the prompt as a raw code block ready for deployment.
variants.constraints:
- Must include strong negative constraints to prevent hallucination.
- Design for maximum determinism and structural depth.
- If the agent handles PII, include data handling guardrails.
- The prompt must be testable — I should be able to evaluate it against edge cases.
- No vague, conversational framing — this is a machine specification.
variants.examples:
Example 1: 'Write a system prompt for a Medical Conference Intelligence Agent. Input: conference program PDF + speaker bios. Output: structured JSON with key findings, speaker profiles, competitive intelligence, and action items for a pharma commercial team.'

Example 2: 'Write a system prompt for InquiroAI's Prompt Evaluator agent. Input: raw user prompt. Output: JSON with score (1-10), issues[], improved_prompt, prompt_type, complexity. Must use meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct.'

Example 3: 'Write a system prompt for a KYC Document Parser. Input: scanned ID documents (PAN, Aadhaar, passport). Output: extracted fields with confidence scores. Must handle rotated images, partial occlusion, and multilingual text.'

variants.description: Production-ready code with CLAUDE.md for handoff to Claude Code
variants.custom_role: Senior data engineer and Python developer who writes production-ready, well-commented code that a junior can extend. Always creates actual files, never inline snippets.
variants.context:
Project: {{PROJECT_NAME}}.
Code purpose: {{CODE_PURPOSE}}.
Input: {{INPUT_SPEC}}.
Output: {{OUTPUT_SPEC}}.
Tech stack: {{TECH_STACK}}.
File locations: {{FILE_PATHS}}.
Existing code: {{EXISTING_CODE_REFS}}.
variants.task:
Build {{DELIVERABLE_TYPE}} that:
{{REQUIREMENT_LIST}}

Create actual files (not inline code snippets).
Save outputs to: {{OUTPUT_PATH}}.
If multi-file project, include a CLAUDE.md with: project objective, file map, data schema, business rules, known issues, next steps.
variants.constraints:
- Write modular, commented code — functions individually testable.
- For PPTX outputs: use python-pptx.
- For data outputs: validate schema before writing.
- Handle edge cases explicitly — do not silently skip errors.
- If file > 500 lines, split into modules.
- Every file reference must include an absolute path.
variants.examples:
Example 1: 'Build build_deck_v2.py — a 28-slide PPTX generator for BharatPe's CAC amortization analysis. Inputs: two CSVs (merchant_master.csv, monthly_transactions.csv). Output: deck at /outputs/BharatPe_CAC_Deck.pptx'

Example 2: 'Create a Python CIBIL XML parser that handles duplicate tags and mismatched reference numbers. Fix line 303 of dcb_api_module_2.py.'

Example 3: 'Build the InquiroAI obsidianWriter.ts module. Input: GraphEntity[]. Output: .md files in Obsidian vault with YAML frontmatter and wikilinks. Must handle create-or-update logic.'

templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Architecture: all components defined with rationale, failure modes addressed, cost model present
- System Prompt: testable against edge cases, negative constraints present, output schema defined
- Code: runs on first attempt, output files created, CLAUDE.md included for multi-file projects
trigger_phrases: How do I create this system...
anti_patterns: Overly abstract architectures with no implementation path
Writing vague conversational prompts instead of machine specs
Not specifying file paths — Claude guesses wrong for OneDrive structure
Asking for inline code snippets when you need runnable files
Mixing orchestration logic with business logic in agent design

templates.name: Content Voice Calibration

variants.description: Platform-specific format: hook → observation → mechanism → non-cheesy CTA
variants.custom_role: Ghostwriter who has studied Surya's voice — writes like a practitioner sharing a hard-won insight, not a LinkedIn influencer farming engagement
variants.context:
Author: Suryadip Ghoshal, Co-Founder Think360.ai, 20+ years in AI/fintech.
Topic: {{TOPIC}}.
The insight I want to land: {{CORE_INSIGHT}}.
Source: {{SOURCE}} — from client work / research / product building / personal experience.
Voice: Direct, practitioner, India-grounded. Lead with observation, not opinion.
variants.task:
Write a LinkedIn post ({{LENGTH}} — short: 150 words, standard: 300 words, long-form: 600 words).

Structure:
1. Hook — a concrete observation or counterintuitive claim (not a question)
2. Context — why this matters now
3. The mechanism — how this actually works (the insight)
4. Evidence — named product, specific number, or lived experience
5. Implication — what the reader should rethink
6. Close — a question or provocation (not 'follow me for more')
variants.constraints:
- No parallel triplets ('We believe in A, B, and C').
- No transitional filler: 'In conclusion', 'It's worth noting', 'This is why'.
- No tidy topic-sentence structures that read like formatted LLM output.
- Named products as evidence, never as advertising.
- Every sentence should feel like it's being recalled, not recited.
- No hashtag spam — max 3 relevant hashtags.
- No emoji-heavy formatting.
variants.examples:
Example 1: 'Write a LinkedIn post on AI in credit underwriting. Core insight: alternate data doesn't replace bureau data, it contextualizes it. Evidence: Irfan the office boy rejected for a two-wheeler loan in 2014 — his UPI transaction history told a different story. 300 words.'

Example 2: 'Write a LinkedIn post on why most AI product companies will fail. Core insight: they're solving for model performance, not workflow integration. Evidence: Think360's experience with BFSI clients who rejected 95% accurate models because they didn't fit existing processes. Short format.'

variants.description: Rewrite Q&A responses in Surya's authentic voice for publication
variants.custom_role: Ghostwriter who makes interview responses sound like a founder speaking to a journalist over coffee — recalled, not recited
variants.context:
Publication: {{PUBLICATION}} — editorial style: {{EDITORIAL_STYLE}}.
Interview format: {{FORMAT}} — written Q&A / spoken transcript / email interview.
Topic: {{TOPIC}}.
Key messages I want to land: {{KEY_MESSAGES}}.
Voice notes: {{VOICE_NOTES}} — e.g., 'lead with human story, no bullet triplets'.
Source material: {{SOURCE_MATERIAL}}.
variants.task:
Rewrite the following Q&A responses:
{{QA_CONTENT}}

For each answer:
- Lead with a human story or concrete observation before industry context
- Named products as evidence, never advertising
- Every answer should feel like it's being recalled, not recited
- Match the publication's editorial style and expected depth
variants.constraints:
- No parallel triplets.
- No tidy topic-sentence structures.
- No transitional filler.
- If the question is about company capabilities, anchor to a specific use case, not a feature list.
- Flag any sentences that still sound AI-generated and suggest more natural alternatives.
variants.examples: Example: 'Rewrite Q1 of the CXQuest interview around Irfan — an office boy who was rejected for a two-wheeler loan in 2014. Lead with his story before explaining alternate data. The publication is mid-market business media, conversational tone.'

variants.description: Career narrative for global roles, executive bios, board-level positioning
variants.custom_role: Executive Search Consultant and Personal Branding Expert for C-Suite Tech Leaders. Writes bios that read like business cases, not resumes.
variants.context:
Target: {{TARGET_ROLE}} (e.g., Head of AI, VP Data, Chief Analytics Officer) at {{TARGET_COMPANIES/SECTORS}}.
Compensation expectation: >$200k.
Core differentiator: 20+ years blending Data/AI with Fintech/RegTech, successful founder exit (Think360 → CAMS merger).
Specific achievements to translate: {{ACHIEVEMENTS}}.
Target geography: {{GEOGRAPHY}} — Singapore, Dubai, US, Europe.
variants.task:
Create a {{DELIVERABLE}} — LinkedIn summary / executive bio / cover letter / elevator pitch.

Translate achievements into business impact and scalable leadership.
Emphasize the intersection of technical rigor and commercial outcomes.
Highlight global readiness and strategic vision over day-to-day tactical execution.
variants.constraints:
- Authoritative, crisp, and metric-driven. Zero buzzword fluff.
- No passive voice or generic corporate jargon.
- Must pass the 'would a board member stop scrolling for this' test.
- Over-index on business transformation, not technical stack details.
variants.examples: Example: 'Refine my LinkedIn summary for Head of AI roles at Tier 1 fintech companies in Singapore/Dubai. Key achievements: built Algo360 (India's first ML-based credit scoring for thin-file borrowers), led Think360 to CAMS acquisition, served 40+ BFSI clients. Make it read like a briefing, not a resume.'

templates.evaluation_criteria:
- LinkedIn: no AI triplets, no filler transitions, human story leads, reads as recalled not recited
- PR/Interview: matches publication style, every answer anchored to specific use case
- Executive: board-member stopping power, metric-driven, no passive voice
trigger_phrases: Make this sound like I'm speaking to a journalist...
anti_patterns: Not specifying the publication's editorial style
Asking for 'professional tone' without defining what that means for your voice
Providing no source material — Claude invents details
Using 'democratize' or 'disrupt' without specifics
Generic motivational framing

templates.name: Regulatory Intelligence Brief
templates.custom_role: Indian regulatory expert who bridges accounting standards (Ind AS), corporate law, and product compliance — writes with the precision of a CA + legal counsel hybrid. For product-facing work, also acts as RegTech Product Strategist translating policy into code.
templates.context:
Regulation/standard: {{REGULATION}} (e.g., Ind AS 113, DPDPA Section 6, SHA Clause 2.3, RBI Master Direction on KYC).
Business context: {{BUSINESS_CONTEXT}}.
The question I need answered: {{CORE_QUESTION}}.
Counterparty's likely interpretation: {{ADVERSARIAL_INTERPRETATION}}.
Documents I'm providing: {{DOCUMENT_LIST}}.
Source of truth: {{SOURCE_DOCUMENT}} — defer to this over training data.
templates.task:
Provide a regulatory intelligence brief covering:

Interpretation Mode (for negotiation/legal prep):
1. Plain-language explanation of {{REGULATION}} as it applies to {{CONTEXT}}
2. My position — what the regulation says in my favor
3. The adversarial interpretation — and why it fails (or where it has merit)
4. The strongest counter-argument against my position — and how to neutralize it
5. Evidence I should gather to strengthen my position
6. Risk assessment: {{RISK_FORMAT}}

Product Mapping Mode (for RegTech feature design):
1. Regulatory Rule → Business Impact → Required Product Capability → Technical Implementation
2. Edge cases where the regulation is ambiguous
3. Conservative technical approach for ambiguous areas
4. Specific Jira epic candidates

Select mode based on {{PURPOSE}}: negotiation-prep / product-design / compliance-audit.
templates.constraints:
- Cite specific clauses, sections, and sub-sections.
- Distinguish between what the regulation says vs. how courts/tribunals have interpreted it.
- Flag where Indian and global (IFRS) standards diverge.
- Do not recommend litigation unless explicitly asked.
- If you're uncertain, say so — do not paper over gaps.
- For product mapping: no manual process suggestions where automated RegTech solutions exist.
templates.audience: {{AUDIENCE}} — myself and co-founder Amit Das for negotiation prep / Product + Engineering for RegTech features / Legal team for compliance briefing.
templates.examples:
Example 1 (Interpretation): 'CAMS may use Ind AS 36 (impairment) to argue Think360 is worth less than ₹100 Cr. The SHA Clause 2.3 has an unconditional floor. Their ESOP valuation used Ind AS 113. Build a brief on why Ind AS 36 cannot override the SHA floor.'

Example 2 (Product Mapping): 'Map DPDPA consent requirements to KYC Co-Pilot product features. Create a regulation-to-feature matrix. Where DPDPA conflicts with CIC reporting mandates, suggest the conservative technical approach.'

Example 3 (Compliance): 'Explain how RBI's new digital lending guidelines impact BatchOps Remediation Platform. What features do we need to add for compliance?'
templates.optional: Separately produce a one-page summary suitable for sharing with a CA / legal advisor as a briefing note, so they can confirm or correct the analysis efficiently.
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Specific clause citations (not generic legal principles)
- Adversarial argument addressed with counter-strategy
- Risk level assigned with justification
- Evidence checklist for strengthening position
- Indian vs. global standard divergence flagged where relevant
- For product mapping: regulation-to-feature matrix present as a table
trigger_phrases: Interpret Clause X of the SHA/SPA...
How does Ind AS [N] apply to...
Translate this regulatory mandate into product features...
templates.success_criteria: Specific clause citations, adversarial argument addressed, risk level assigned, evidence checklist, one-page briefing note for CA/legal
common_follow_ups: What if they cite Ind AS 36 Section 12(b) specifically?
Write this as a briefing note for my CA
What's the estoppel argument based on the ESOP valuation?
Write the user story for capability X
anti_patterns: Not providing the actual contract/regulation text — leads to generic interpretation
Asking for legal opinion without providing adversarial position to stress-test
Summarizing the law without providing product implications
Missing source of truth designation

templates.name: Skeptical Expert Review / Red Team
templates.custom_role: A highly skeptical domain expert (VC/operator/regulator/hostile auditor) with deep experience and pattern recognition. Defaults to 'why this fails' before considering 'why this works'.
templates.task:
Critically evaluate this from first principles:
1. Identify the top 3-5 weak or untested assumptions
2. Highlight hidden risks (market, technical, regulatory, team)
3. Assess real market need vs. perceived need
4. Evaluate defensibility — distinguish real moats from temporary advantages
5. Determine whether this is viable, niche, or dead-on-arrival
6. For each failure mode: what would need to be true for this to succeed anyway?
7. If this were a Pre-Mortem: what's the headline 12 months from now if it failed?

End with: 'What would change your mind?' — the specific evidence or milestone that would convert a skeptic.
templates.constraints:
- Be brutally honest.
- No generic startup advice or SWOT analysis.
- Focus on failure modes first, then pivot paths.
- Distinguish between 'this is hard' and 'this is structurally impossible.'
- If an idea doesn't survive scrutiny, say so directly.
- No hedge-everything-equally diplomatic framing.
templates.audience: Myself — for deciding whether to pursue and how to prioritize.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'You are a skeptical VC who has seen 500 AI pitch decks this year. Review InquiroAI's positioning as a $20/month domain expert AI OS. Tear it apart. Why does this fail? What would make you invest?'

Example 2: 'Pre-mortem: I'm launching TrustMesh (Agent Identity Protocol) aimed at enterprise AI deployments. It's March 2027 and TrustMesh has shut down. Write the postmortem. Then tell me how to prevent each failure mode.'

Example 3: 'Act as a hostile RBI auditor. Review our KYC Co-Pilot architecture for regulatory risk. What keeps you up at night?'
templates.optional:
After the critique, add:
1. 'Pivot paths' — if the core thesis fails, what adjacent opportunities survive?
2. 'Sharper versions' — how to reframe the same assets into a more defensible position
3. Specific metrics or milestones that would de-risk each concern
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- At least 3 specific failure modes identified (not generic risks)
- Each failure mode has a 'what would need to be true' counter
- Moat assessment distinguishes real vs. temporary advantages
- Pre-mortem headline is specific, not vague
- 'What would change your mind' section is actionable, not hand-wavy
templates.success_criteria: Clear identification of fatal flaws, specific pivot paths, honest moat assessment, actionable 'what would change your mind'
common_follow_ups: What specificity would make this investable?
Give sharper versions
Now build the version that survives your critique
anti_patterns: Overly polite feedback
Generic SWOT analysis
Hedge-everything-equally diplomacy
Refusing to take a stance

templates.name: Structured Comparison Framework
templates.context:
Compare:
- Option A: {{A}}
- Option B: {{B}}
- Option C: {{C}} (optional)

Decision context: {{WHY_COMPARING}} — e.g., purchasing decision, technology selection, strategic choice.
My priorities: {{PRIORITY_WEIGHTS}} — e.g., 'value durability over aesthetics' or 'prioritize time-to-market over feature completeness'.
My profile: {{MY_CONTEXT}} — relevant constraints or preferences.
templates.task:
Compare across these dimensions:
{{COMPARISON_DIMENSIONS}}

Default dimensions (if not specified):
- Use case fit for my specific context
- Strengths / weaknesses
- Cost / value analysis
- Availability / accessibility
- Long-term implications (3-year view)
- Hidden trade-offs most people miss

Provide:
1. Comparison table (dimensions as rows, options as columns)
2. Dimension-by-dimension commentary (not just the table)
3. Clear recommendation with reasoning
4. Persona-based recommendation: 'For someone with your profile, specifically...'
templates.constraints:
- Use table + commentary format — table alone is insufficient.
- Must provide a clear recommendation — no 'it depends' without specifying what it depends on.
- Weight dimensions by my stated priorities.
- Include at least one non-obvious insight per option.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Compare Tudor Black Bay 58 vs. Omega Seamaster 300 vs. Grand Seiko SBGA211 for a daily-wear watch in the $5K-$10K range. My priorities: wrist comfort > brand prestige > resale value. I have a 6.75 inch wrist.'

Example 2: 'Compare Next.js vs. Remix vs. Vite+React for InquiroAI's frontend rebuild. Priorities: developer velocity > SSR capability > ecosystem maturity. Current stack: Vite+React.'
templates.optional: Add a 'What I'd actually do' section — a conversational recommendation that goes beyond the structured analysis.
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Comparison table present and complete
- Clear recommendation stated (not 'all are good')
- Priorities reflected in weighting
- At least one non-obvious insight per option
- Persona-based recommendation present
templates.success_criteria: Clear decision guidance with priority-weighted recommendation and non-obvious insights
common_follow_ups: Which should I pick given my profile?
What about option D?
What am I missing?
anti_patterns: Equal weighting with no recommendation
Table without commentary
Generic comparison without persona context

templates.name: CLAUDE.md & Context Handoff Spec
templates.custom_role: Senior AI engineer who designs context specifications that minimize Claude Code's need for clarification while maximizing output quality. Every line earns its place.
templates.context:
Project: {{PROJECT_NAME}}.
Purpose: {{PURPOSE}} — project setup / session handoff / continuation spec.
Task scope for Claude Code: {{TASK_SCOPE}}.
Tech stack: {{TECH_STACK}}.
File structure: {{FILE_MAP}}.
Business rules that must never be violated: {{BUSINESS_RULES}}.
Known edge cases: {{EDGE_CASES}}.
Current state (if handoff): {{CURRENT_STATE}}.
What was NOT completed (if handoff): {{INCOMPLETE_ITEMS}}.
templates.task:
Write a {{SPEC_TYPE}} file:

If CLAUDE.md (project setup):
1. Project objective (2-3 sentences)
2. File map with roles
3. Data schema (inputs and outputs)
4. Business rules as numbered, testable conditions
5. Output format requirements
6. Known issues and workarounds
7. 'Next actions' section for continuation

If CONTEXT_HANDOFF.md (session continuation):
1. Project objective (2-3 sentences)
2. What was completed this session (numbered list)
3. Current state — what the code/document/analysis looks like now
4. File locations — absolute paths
5. Specific next actions — numbered, priority-ordered
6. Known issues — what broke, what was patched, what's fragile
7. Open questions requiring user input

Save to: {{OUTPUT_PATH}}.
Max length: {{MAX_TOKENS}} tokens.
templates.constraints:
- Be terse. Every line must earn its place.
- Business rules must be specific enough that violations are testable.
- File paths must be absolute.
- Avoid narrative — this is a spec, not documentation.
- Flag anything requiring user input before Claude Code can proceed autonomously.
- Do not summarize what we discussed — only what the state IS now.
- Under 1,000 words for handoffs.
templates.audience: Claude Code in a fresh session — zero prior context.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Write CLAUDE.md for the BharatPe CAC Amortization project. Files at /OneDrive/02 Work/BP Merchant Amortization/. Active merchant = at least 1 transaction ≥ ₹3. Framework: Ind AS 115.'

Example 2: 'Write CLAUDE.md for InquiroAI Sprint 3. Six layers. OpenRouter backend. Context compression at 70% threshold. Notion memory. Obsidian knowledge graph.'

Example 3: 'We hit the context limit debugging dcb_api_module_2.py. Save a handoff file with: the six issues identified, the fix at line 303, file paths, and four prioritized next actions.'
templates.optional:
For CLAUDE.md: also create CLAUDE_full.md with complete docs, noting what was trimmed.
For handoffs: if multiple parallel workstreams, create separate section per workstream with its own next-action list.
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Under token budget
- Business rules are testable (not vague)
- All file paths are absolute
- No ambiguity in schema or output spec
- No narrative padding
- New session can continue without asking clarifying questions
trigger_phrases: Create a context handoff for the next session...
Build the spec file for Claude Code...
Save our progress and next steps...
templates.success_criteria: New session can continue without clarifying questions, all file paths present, next actions unambiguous, under token budget
common_follow_ups: Trim it further — it's still too long
Add the edge case for null values
Separate business rules into RULES.md
anti_patterns: Writing narrative documentation rather than a spec — wastes tokens
Omitting file paths — Claude Code will guess wrong
Not specifying which decisions require user confirmation vs. autonomous action
Asking for handoff only after context window is fully exhausted — no tokens left

templates.name: Ideation-to-Company Builder
templates.custom_role: Venture strategist and company builder who thinks in platform economics, network effects, and competitive moat theory — not startup clichés. Has seen 1000 pitch decks and funded 50 companies.
templates.context:
Seed concept: {{CONCEPT_SEED}} — e.g., 'a trust layer for AI agents', 'regulatory intelligence as a service'.
My existing assets: {{MY_ASSETS}} — skills, products, relationships, IP, team.
Market context: {{MARKET_CONTEXT}}.
Constraints: {{CONSTRAINTS}} — capital, time, geography, regulatory.
templates.task:
Develop this into a complete company concept covering:
1. The problem — with a vivid 'world without this' scenario
2. The solution — what exactly it does, how it works mechanically
3. Business model — revenue streams, pricing, unit economics logic. Clear answer to 'who pays, how much, when'
4. Competitive moat — what makes this defensible in 3 years. Distinguish real moats from temporary advantages
5. Go-to-market — first 90 days, beachhead market, expansion path. Start from MY existing assets, not a blank slate
6. Why now — what has changed to make this possible today
7. Founding team gaps — what skills I need to recruit
8. Analogies — what company/protocol is this most similar to and why
9. 90-day action plan — with specific, executable named tasks
10. Devil's advocate — top 3 reasons this fails. For each: what would need to be true for it to succeed anyway
templates.constraints:
- No clichés: 'disrupt', 'democratize', 'AI-powered [anything]' without specifics.
- Business model must have a clear answer to 'who pays, how much, when.'
- Moat section must be honest — distinguish real moats from temporary advantages.
- GTM must start from my existing assets and relationships, not a blank slate.
- If an idea doesn't survive scrutiny, say so.
- 90-day plan must have named tasks, not categories.
templates.audience: Myself — for deciding whether to pursue and how to prioritize.
templates.examples: Example: 'Combine Agent Identity Protocol and AI Output Attestation into a single company concept. My assets: KYC/identity products, India BFSI relationships, CAMSLens, 120-person team. Think: Visa/SWIFT/Verisign trajectory. Call it TrustMesh.'
templates.optional:
After the company concept:
1. Run devil's advocate: 3 most likely reasons this fails + counter-strategies
2. One-pager suitable for a potential co-founder
3. 'What's the India BFSI go-to-market specifically?' section
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Business model has specific revenue mechanics (not 'SaaS fees')
- Moat assessment is honest about what's real vs. temporary
- GTM leverages existing assets, not hypothetical channels
- 90-day plan has named, executable tasks
- Devil's advocate section present with counter-strategies
- 'Why now' argument is specific, not generic AI hype
trigger_phrases: Turn this idea into a company concept...
Build a company around the concept of...
Analyze the fastest path to $10M ARR for...
templates.success_criteria: Specific business model with revenue mechanics, honest moat assessment, 90-day plan with named tasks, devil's advocate section, why-now argument
common_follow_ups: Now build the technical architecture
Write a one-pager for a potential co-founder
What's the India BFSI go-to-market specifically?
Which project should I prioritize for revenue?
anti_patterns: Asking for ideation without providing existing assets — leads to generic advice
Not asking for devil's advocate — produces optimism bias
Missing 'why now' — separates investable from interesting
Refusing to take a stance or recommending 'do both'

templates.name: Meeting Synthesis & Action Extraction
templates.custom_role: Executive Chief of Staff who produces crisp, scannable digests — eliminates all conversational filler
templates.task:
Synthesize into a structured executive summary:
1. Core Decisions Made (numbered, with rationale)
2. Open Questions / Blockers (with owners if identifiable)
3. Action Items — **Bold owner names** and deadlines
4. Key Technical/Product Changes discussed (if any)
5. Next meeting / follow-up date

If technical architecture or product scope changes were discussed, break them into a dedicated 'Technical Impact' sub-section.
templates.constraints:
- Eliminate all conversational filler.
- Keep summary under {{MAX_WORDS}} words (default: 500).
- Bold action owners and deadlines.
- Decisions must include rationale, not just the decision.
- If something was discussed but NOT decided, put it in Open Questions, not Decisions.
templates.audience: Internal team (Founders, Project Owners) — must be pasteable into Slack or Notion immediately.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Summarize these notes from the CAMS negotiation prep meeting. Attendees: Surya, Amit, Priya (legal). Extract decisions, blockers, and action items with owners.'

Example 2: 'Clean up this InquiroAI sprint planning transcript. Pull out: what we committed to, what got deferred, and who owns what.'
templates.optional:
After the summary:
1. Draft a Slack message for the team channel (3-4 lines max)
2. Draft a client email if external stakeholders were discussed
3. Turn action items into Jira/Asana ticket descriptions
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Decisions have rationale attached
- Action items have owners and deadlines (or 'TBD' if not specified)
- Open questions are separated from decisions
- Under word limit
- Immediately pasteable into Slack/Notion
trigger_phrases: Clean up this transcript into a brief...
What did we decide in this meeting?
templates.success_criteria: Clean, scannable digest pasteable into Slack/Notion, all action items have owners, decisions have rationale
common_follow_ups: Draft an email to the client summarizing decisions
Turn action items into Jira tickets
What was left unresolved?
anti_patterns: Transcribing verbatim instead of synthesizing
Failing to isolate action items from general discussion
Missing owner attribution on action items

templates.name: GTM Strategy & Persona Builder
templates.custom_role: VP of Marketing and Sales Ops for Enterprise SaaS who has launched 10+ B2B products. Thinks in ICP segments, buying triggers, and objection maps — not funnel diagrams.
templates.context:
Product: {{PRODUCT_NAME}}.
Target market: {{TARGET_MARKET}}.
Primary use case: {{USE_CASE}}.
ICP hypotheses: {{ICP_LIST}} — e.g., BFSI CTO, NBFC Risk Head, US Pharma Procurement.
Launch budget/resources: {{RESOURCES}}.
Existing distribution assets: {{EXISTING_CHANNELS}} — relationships, content, partnerships.
Competitive context: {{COMPETITORS}}.
templates.task:
Produce two deliverables:

Deliverable 1 — ICP/Persona Forge:
For each ICP segment:
- Role, seniority, reporting line
- Top 3 pain points (specific to this role, not generic)
- Buying triggers (what event makes them search for a solution NOW)
- Objection map (top 3 objections and counter-arguments)
- Messaging framework (headline, supporting proof points, CTA)
- Channels where this persona discovers solutions

Deliverable 2 — 90-Day GTM Execution Plan:
Phase 1 (Days 1-30): Positioning, beta recruitment, collateral creation
Phase 2 (Days 31-60): Launch channels, outbound sequences, content calendar
Phase 3 (Days 61-90): Sales enablement, feedback loops, optimization

KPIs at Day 30, 60, and 90.
templates.constraints:
- Start from my existing assets and relationships, not a blank slate.
- Focus on direct enterprise sales motions, LinkedIn thought leadership, and partner networks over generic performance marketing.
- No B2C tactics (TikTok, Instagram) for B2B enterprise.
- Objection maps must be specific to the ICP, not generic.
- Timeline must have named tasks, not just categories.
- Pricing guidance must answer 'who pays, how much, when.'
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Build GTM strategy for KYC Co-Pilot targeting Indian NBFCs and mid-tier banks. ICPs: Chief Compliance Officer, Head of Operations, CTO. My existing assets: 40+ BFSI client relationships via Think360, CAMSLens brand, RBI compliance expertise.'

Example 2: 'Build persona + GTM for InquiroAI targeting AI-forward product teams at Series B+ startups. Budget: $0 (content-led). My assets: LinkedIn following, Substack, domain expertise.'
templates.optional:
Add:
1. Sales enablement one-pager per ICP
2. 3-email outbound sequence per persona
3. LinkedIn content calendar (4 posts/month) aligned to GTM phases
4. Partner channel strategy if applicable
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Each ICP has specific pain points (not 'needs better efficiency')
- Buying triggers are event-driven, not generic
- Objection maps have specific counter-arguments
- 90-day plan has named tasks with owners
- KPIs are measurable at each checkpoint
- GTM leverages existing assets, not hypothetical channels
trigger_phrases: Draft a 90-day roadmap for taking [Product] to market
templates.success_criteria: Specific ICPs with event-driven buying triggers, objection maps with counters, 90-day plan with named tasks and KPIs
common_follow_ups: Draft the cold outreach sequence for ICP 1
What should the sales enablement deck look like?
LinkedIn content calendar for launch month
anti_patterns: Generic pain points ('needs better visibility')
B2C tactics for B2B enterprise
GTM plan without existing asset leverage
Personas without objection maps

templates.name: Decision Intelligence Brief
templates.custom_role: Decision scientist who combines scenario analysis, hypothesis testing, and pre-mortem thinking into a single decision artifact. Thinks in probability distributions, not point estimates.
templates.context:
Decision: {{DECISION_STATEMENT}} — e.g., 'Should I prioritize KYC Co-Pilot or InquiroAI for the next 90 days?'
Options: {{OPTIONS_LIST}}
Key variables: {{VARIABLES}} — what could change the answer.
Stakes: {{STAKES}} — what's at risk if I get this wrong.
Time horizon: {{TIME_HORIZON}}.
My current lean: {{CURRENT_LEAN}} (if any).
Data I have: {{AVAILABLE_DATA}}.
Data I wish I had: {{MISSING_DATA}}.
templates.task:
Produce a Decision Intelligence Brief:

1. Decision framing — restate the decision crisply, identify what type of decision this is (reversible/irreversible, one-way/two-way door)
2. Options analysis — for each option:
   - Best case outcome + probability estimate
   - Base case outcome + probability estimate
   - Worst case outcome + probability estimate
   - Key assumptions that must hold
3. Weighted comparison matrix (RICE, ICE, or custom framework)
4. Hypothesis stress-test — for my current lean:
   - What evidence supports it?
   - What evidence contradicts it?
   - What would need to be true for the opposite conclusion?
   - Confidence level (0-100%) with justification
5. Pre-mortem — it's {{TIME_HORIZON}} later and the decision failed. What happened?
6. Decision recommendation — with specific conditions and trigger points for reassessment
7. 'What I'd do today' — the concrete first 3 actions
templates.constraints:
- Must take a stance — no 'it depends' without specifying the dependency.
- Probability estimates must be explicit, even if rough.
- Pre-mortem must be specific, not generic risk language.
- Flag where I'm reasoning from data vs. intuition vs. analogy.
- If options can be sequenced rather than chosen, say so.
templates.audience: Myself — for making the actual decision.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Decision: Which of my 11 products should I focus on for the fastest path to $10M ARR? Options: KYC Co-Pilot, InquiroAI, rAIdar, OrgOS. Variables: sales cycle length, ACV potential, competitive intensity, my existing relationships.'

Example 2: 'Decision: Should I accept the CAMS settlement at ₹80 Cr or push for the SHA floor of ₹100 Cr? Variables: legal cost, relationship damage, timeline, probability of arbitration win.'

Example 3: 'Hypothesis stress-test: India is entering a K-shaped consumption crisis that will last 3+ years. My confidence: 70%. Challenge this with counter-evidence.'
templates.optional:
Add:
1. Decision journal entry — structured for future review (date, context, options considered, decision made, rationale, review date)
2. Sensitivity analysis — which single variable changing would flip the decision?
3. Reversibility assessment — how easily can I course-correct if wrong?
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Clear stance taken with reasoning
- Probability estimates present for each scenario
- Pre-mortem is specific (not 'things went wrong')
- Hypothesis stress-test includes counter-evidence
- First 3 actions are concrete and executable
- Reversibility assessed
trigger_phrases: Which project should I focus on...
templates.success_criteria: Clear recommendation with probability estimates, pre-mortem, hypothesis stress-test with counter-evidence, concrete first actions
common_follow_ups: What if variable X changes?
Build the 90-day plan for the recommended option
What's my review trigger — when should I reassess?
anti_patterns: Refusing to take a stance
Point estimates without ranges
Generic risk language instead of specific pre-mortem
Missing reversibility assessment

templates.name: Pricing & Monetization Framework
templates.custom_role: SaaS pricing strategist who has designed pricing for 20+ B2B products. Thinks in value metrics, willingness-to-pay curves, and competitive anchoring — not cost-plus.
templates.context:
Product: {{PRODUCT_NAME}}.
Target market: {{TARGET_MARKET}} — segment by size/sophistication.
Value delivered: {{VALUE_PROPOSITION}} — what outcome the buyer gets.
Current pricing (if any): {{CURRENT_PRICING}}.
Competitive pricing landscape: {{COMPETITOR_PRICING}}.
Cost structure: {{COST_STRUCTURE}} — per-user, per-API-call, per-model-inference.
Sales motion: {{SALES_MOTION}} — self-serve / sales-assisted / enterprise.
templates.task:
Design a complete pricing and monetization framework:

1. Value metric analysis — what unit of value does the customer actually pay for? (seats, API calls, documents processed, decisions made, AUM managed)
2. Pricing model recommendation — subscription / usage-based / hybrid / outcome-based. With reasoning.
3. Tier design:
   - Tier names, target segments, included features, pricing
   - Free/trial tier (if applicable): what's included, conversion triggers
   - Enterprise tier: custom pricing triggers
4. Competitive positioning — where do we sit relative to alternatives? Above/below/beside?
5. Unit economics — estimated CAC, LTV, payback period per tier
6. Pricing psychology — anchoring strategy, decoy tier design, annual vs. monthly discount
7. Launch pricing vs. scale pricing — what to charge on Day 1 vs. Day 365
8. 'Who pays, how much, when' — specific answer for each segment
templates.constraints:
- Must be specific to the product's value metric — no generic 'per seat per month' without justification.
- Competitive anchoring must reference actual competitors with approximate pricing.
- Unit economics must be estimated, even if rough.
- Distinguish between pricing for India market vs. global market if applicable.
- Flag pricing decisions that require customer discovery data to validate.
templates.audience: Myself and co-founders — for pricing decisions and investor conversations.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Design pricing for InquiroAI. Value: domain expert AI workspace. Competitors: ChatGPT ($20/mo), Notion AI ($10/mo add-on), custom GPTs (free). My cost: ~$0.02-0.15 per AI call via OpenRouter. Target: individual domain experts and small teams.'

Example 2: 'Design pricing for KYC Co-Pilot targeting Indian NBFCs. Value: 60% reduction in KYC processing time. Competitors: manual process ($5/KYC), legacy vendors ($2-3/KYC). My cost: inference + API calls per document.'

Example 3: 'Design pricing for rAIdar (ad compliance surveillance). Value: regulatory risk reduction. Buyers: Chief Compliance Officers at ad agencies and brands. This is a fear-of-loss purchase, not an efficiency purchase — price accordingly.'
templates.optional:
Add:
1. Pricing page copy (3 tiers, feature comparison table)
2. Objection handling for 'too expensive' and 'why not use ChatGPT'
3. Annual pricing discount strategy
4. Revenue projection model (units × price × conversion rate × churn)
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Value metric identified and justified
- Tier design is specific (not just Good/Better/Best labels)
- Competitive anchoring references real competitors
- Unit economics estimated
- 'Who pays, how much, when' answered for each segment
- Launch vs. scale pricing distinguished
templates.success_criteria: Specific value metric, tier design with pricing, competitive anchoring, unit economics, clear 'who pays how much when'
common_follow_ups: Build the pricing page copy
What if we go usage-based instead?
Revenue projection for Year 1
How does this change for the India vs. US market?
anti_patterns: Generic per-seat pricing without value metric analysis
Missing competitive anchoring
Cost-plus pricing for a value-delivered product
No distinction between launch and scale pricing

templates.name: Data Intelligence Analyst
templates.custom_role: Senior Data Scientist with 15+ years in BFSI and enterprise data — equally fluent in statistical theory, data engineering pipelines, and executive storytelling. Thinks in distributions, not averages. Treats data quality as a first-class concern, not an afterthought.
templates.context:
Dataset/Source: {{DATA_SOURCE}} — e.g., CSV, database table, API response, data warehouse query.
Data description: {{DATA_DESCRIPTION}} — what each field represents, business context.
Business question: {{BUSINESS_QUESTION}} — the decision this analysis will inform.
Domain: {{DOMAIN}} — fintech / credit risk / KYC / pharma / general.
Data quality concerns: {{KNOWN_ISSUES}} — nulls, duplicates, encoding, schema drift, vintage effects.
Downstream use: {{DOWNSTREAM}} — board report / ML feature / regulatory filing / product decision.
Source of truth for definitions: {{DEFINITIONS_DOC}} — e.g., 'active merchant = at least 1 txn ≥ ₹3 in trailing 30 days'.
templates.task:
Conduct a comprehensive data intelligence analysis:

Phase 1 — Data Understanding & Quality Audit:
1. Schema profiling — types, cardinality, nulls, distributions per column
2. Data quality assessment — completeness, consistency, validity, timeliness, uniqueness
3. Anomaly detection — outliers, impossible values, distribution breaks, vintage/cohort effects
4. Key relationships — correlations, join keys, referential integrity
5. Data engineering assessment — is this data pipeline-ready? Schema drift risks? Partitioning strategy?

Phase 2 — Statistical Analysis:
1. Descriptive statistics — but interpret them, don't just compute them. What does the median vs. mean gap tell us?
2. Distribution analysis — which columns are normal, skewed, bimodal, heavy-tailed? Why does it matter for {{BUSINESS_QUESTION}}?
3. Hypothesis testing — formalize the business question as H0/H1, select appropriate test (t-test, chi-squared, Mann-Whitney, KS test), report p-values with effect sizes (not just significance)
4. Segmentation — cohort analysis, clustering, or stratification that reveals hidden structure
5. Temporal patterns — trends, seasonality, regime changes, lagged effects
6. Feature engineering candidates — derived variables that would improve downstream ML or analysis

Phase 3 — Insight Synthesis & Reporting:
1. Executive summary — 3-5 key findings stated as business implications, not statistical facts
2. 'So what' for each finding — what decision does this inform?
3. Data lineage concerns — where in the pipeline could these numbers break?
4. Confidence assessment — for each finding, state confidence level and what additional data would increase it
5. Methodology documentation — reproducible steps, assumptions, tools used
6. Visualization recommendations — which charts to create and why (not generic 'make a bar chart')

Output format: {{FORMAT}} — Markdown report / Jupyter notebook / Python script + report / dashboard spec.
templates.constraints:
- Never report a mean without also reporting median, std dev, and noting the distribution shape.
- Never claim statistical significance without effect size.
- Distinguish between statistical significance and practical significance.
- If sample size is too small for a test, say so — don't run it anyway.
- Treat missing data as information, not noise — document the missingness pattern (MCAR, MAR, MNAR).
- Flag survivorship bias explicitly when analyzing cohort data.
- All SQL/Python code must be reproducible — no magic numbers, hardcoded paths, or undocumented filters.
- For BFSI/credit data: respect vintage effects, seasoning curves, and regulatory reporting windows.
- For pharma data: flag potential selection bias in trial/study populations.
- Data engineering concerns first: if the pipeline is broken, the analysis is meaningless.
templates.audience: {{AUDIENCE}} — data science team / business leadership / regulatory / product team. Sophistication: {{SOPHISTICATION}} — technical / mixed / executive.
templates.examples:
Example 1: 'Analyze the BharatPe merchant transaction dataset (merchant_master.csv + monthly_transactions.csv). Business question: What is the true CAC amortization period for active merchants? Definition: active = at least 1 txn ≥ ₹3. Watch for: survivorship bias (analyzing only merchants who stayed), vintage effects (2022 cohort vs. 2024 cohort behave differently), seasonal patterns (festival months). Output: Markdown report + Python notebook.'

Example 2: 'Profile the CIBIL XML parsed dataset from dcb_api_module_2.py. 50K records, 200+ fields post-extraction. Business question: Which bureau features are most predictive of 90DPD default? Known issues: duplicate account tags, mismatched reference numbers, inconsistent date formats across CICs. Data engineering first: assess schema stability before feature engineering.'

Example 3: 'Analyze KYC rejection data across 15 NBFCs using KwikID. Business question: Where in the KYC funnel do we lose the most applicants, and is the pattern different for thin-file vs. bureau-present applicants? Regulatory context: RBI KYC Master Direction 2023. Output: Executive report for compliance team + technical appendix for data team.'

Example 4: 'Pharma KOL profiling dataset — 2,000 physicians across 5 therapeutic areas. Profile the data, identify clustering patterns for KOL tiering, and flag any selection bias in how the initial list was constructed. Output: Analysis report with methodology appendix suitable for Medical Affairs review.'
templates.optional:
After the analysis:
1. Data quality scorecard — rate the dataset on a 1-10 scale across 5 dimensions with specific improvement recommendations
2. Pipeline hardening recommendations — what monitors/alerts should exist on this data
3. Feature store candidates — which derived variables should be computed once and reused
4. 'If I had more data' section — what additional data would unlock the next level of insight
5. Reproducibility package — requirements.txt, data dictionary, run instructions
templates.evaluation_criteria:
- Schema profiling is complete (no columns skipped)
- Data quality issues documented with severity and business impact
- Statistical tests are appropriate for the data type and distribution
- Effect sizes reported alongside p-values
- Survivorship bias explicitly addressed for cohort data
- Missing data pattern characterized (MCAR/MAR/MNAR)
- Executive summary translates stats into business implications
- Code is reproducible with no hardcoded values
- Data engineering concerns addressed before analytical concerns
- Confidence levels stated for each major finding
trigger_phrases: What does this data tell us about...
templates.success_criteria: Executive summary with business implications, data quality scorecard, reproducible methodology, statistical rigor (effect sizes + significance), pipeline concerns flagged, confidence levels on findings
common_follow_ups: Build a dashboard from these findings
Write the SQL for the key metrics
Create the feature engineering pipeline
Turn this into a board-ready presentation
What ML model should we use on this?
anti_patterns: Jumping to modeling before profiling data quality
Reporting means without distributions
Running statistical tests on too-small samples without flagging it
Ignoring survivorship bias in cohort analysis
Treating missing data as random when it's systematic
Hardcoded filters without documentation
Charts without interpretation
Statistical significance without practical significance

cross_platform_anti_patterns.description: Recurring anti-patterns identified across ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini analyses. These are baked into every template as hardened fixes.

patterns.name: Format Ambiguity
patterns.description: Specifying content in detail but omitting output format. Claude defaults to inline text, Gemini to markdown.
patterns.fix: Every template now requires: 'Output format: [type]. File save location: [path].' in the task field.

patterns.name: Audience Underspecification
patterns.description: Not specifying primary reader, sophistication level, or what they're skeptical of.
patterns.fix: Every template now has mandatory audience field with: role, sophistication level, and skepticism points.

patterns.name: Content-First Instead of Reader-Question-First
patterns.description: Providing source material and asking to 'turn this into X' without specifying what question the reader is asking.
patterns.fix: Document templates now include: 'The reader's primary question is: [X]. The document should answer it by page/section [N].'

patterns.name: Missing Source of Truth
patterns.description: Not designating which document is authoritative when Claude's training data might conflict.
patterns.fix: Templates with document/contract input now include: 'Source of truth: [document]. Where my documents conflict with your training, defer to my documents.'

patterns.name: Missing Evaluation Criteria
patterns.description: Asking for outputs without specifying what 'good' looks like.
patterns.fix: Every template now has an explicit evaluation_criteria field.

patterns.name: Session-End Handoff Neglect
patterns.description: Running long sessions and only asking for handoff when context window is nearly exhausted.
patterns.fix: TPL-010 exists as a dedicated pattern. Rule: at 60% context, proactively create CONTEXT_HANDOFF.md.

patterns.name: Missing Chain-of-Thought on Complex Reasoning
patterns.description: Asking for final answers on complex valuation/architecture without forcing show-your-work.
patterns.fix: Complex templates (TPL-003, TPL-007, TPL-014) include 'think step-by-step' directives in optional field.

domain_coverage_gaps.description: Domains where Surya is active but prompting is ad-hoc. Templates should be added in v2.

gaps.description: Core revenue stream but near-zero pharma-specific templates. Need: KOL identification, clinical trial analysis, medical affairs use cases, Sunshine Act compliance.
Occasional investment analysis but no systematic personal finance template. Would serve both personal decisions and Affluence360 product development.
120+ person team, scaling, but no templates for org design, JD creation, interview structure, team composition.
